audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two colleagues, Joan and Peter, organizing a Christmas dinner for their office staff.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, let's try and get it sorted out today, so we don't have it hanging over us, OK?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I'll take notes.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: First thing, numbers.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Have we got anything definite?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Well, I've been working it out, and I think 40 to 43.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Shall we put 45 to be on the safe side?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Yep.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Joan and Peter agree that 45 is a safe number to book for.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, 45 is written as the answer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the first part of the conversation, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, let's try and get it sorted out today, so we don't have it hanging over us, OK?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Good idea, I'll take notes.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: First thing, numbers.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Have we got anything definite?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Well, I've been working it out, and I think 40 to 43.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Shall we put 45 to be on the safe side?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Yep.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Dates...
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Well, that's straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: The last working day before Christmas... which is...
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: ...which is December the 21st.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: ...which is going to be pretty difficult to book at Christmas,
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: so we'd better think of 2 or 3 places, just to be on the safe side.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Well, last year's was hopeless.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: The Red Lion, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Yep, we ought to go for something more expensive, cos you...
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: ...you get what you pay for.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Hmm, that new Indian restaurant in Wetherfield is supposed to be excellent, the Rajdoot.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Oh, how do you spell that?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: RAJDOOT.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: It's bound to be packed.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Well, let's put that down as the first choice, and have some backups.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: What about the Park View Hotel as a second choice?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's always reliable.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Park View Hotel...
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: And the London Arms in case.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: London arms...
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I'll call them now if you want.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: No, I'll do it, Joan.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: You're really busy.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, have you got the numbers?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Not for the Rajdoot, but right, Park View hotel.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: 777192, and London Arms, 208657.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Great. Before I ring, we'd better just make sure they're within the price range.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Up to £15 a head?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: I think you'll find some people won't be able to go that high.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Well, you can't get anything decent under 10 pounds.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: OK, we'll say 12.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: And we'd better make sure there's good vegetarian food.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: And a non-smoking section, you know what the boss is like.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Don't remind me.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Uh. I'll let you know as soon as I get anything.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good news, I found Rajdoot's number straight away, and they can fit us in.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Their Christmas menu sounds great.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: What is it?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Uh. French onion soup or fruit juice.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Roast dinner or lentil curry,
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: sounds ordinary, but my friend said it was really tasty.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Hmm, lentil curry, that's unusual.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: And then for dessert, there's traditional plum pudding or apple pie, plus coffee.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: That sounds really good for £12. Did you book it?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Well, I said I'd check with the staff first, but they did say they'd hold the booking until next Wednesday anyway.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, and if we go ahead, they'd like a 50 pound deposit.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, 50 is normal.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: That's fine.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: And they want a letter.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Right to confirm.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Oh, and they say with such large numbers.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: We have to choose the menu in advance.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, that won't be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: I'll put up a notice with details of the restaurant and the menu.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: When did you say they wanted confirmation by?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: It was oh, let's see, uh, the fourth of November.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Where do you think I should put up the notice, where everyone's guaranteed to see it?
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: On the cafe notice board, I should think.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Hmm. Hardly anyone looks at that.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Well, the Newsletter is probably your best bet.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, good idea.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: I'll go and do that now.
audioscript.speaker: JOAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a college tutor asking Julie Brooks, director of a college Sports Center, to talk to a group of students about the center.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the first part of the talk, and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, I'll hand over now to Julie Brooks.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Welcome to the Sports Center.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: It's good to see that there are so many people wanting to find out about our sports facilities.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: First of all, membership.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: All students at the college are entitled to become members of the Sports Center, for an annual fee of £9.50.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: To register with us and get your membership card, you need to come to reception between 2 and 6 pm, Monday to Thursday.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: I'm afraid we can't register new members on Friday.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: So it's Monday to Thursday, 2 to 6 at reception.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Now, there are three things that you must remember to bring with you when you come to register.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: They are your union card, a recent passport-sized photograph of yourself, and the fee.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: It doesn't matter whether you bring cash or a cheque.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: We can't issue your card, unless you bring all three.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: So don't forget your union card, passport photo, and fee.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Then once you've got your sports card, you will need to bring it with you whenever you come to book or use any Sports Center facilities.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Booking over the phone is not allowed.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: So you have to come here in person with your card when you want to book.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Our opening hours seem to get longer every year.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: We are now open from 9 am to 10 pm on weekdays, and from 10 am to 6 pm on Saturdays.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: For those of you who are up and about early in the morning, we're introducing a 50% morning discount this year.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: This is because the facilities tended to be underused in the mornings last year.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: It means that all the sessions will be half price between 9:00 am and 12:00 noon on weekdays.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, what exactly are the facilities?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: What sports can you play here?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Well, this room we're in at the moment is called the main hall.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: And it's used mainly for team sports such as football, volleyball and basketball, but also for badminton and aerobics.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: On the other side of the reception area there's the dance studio.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: This provides a smaller, more intimate space, which we use for ballet, modern dance and martial arts, not at the same time of course.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: Then, in a separate building, which you may have noticed on your way here, it's on the other side of the car park, there are the squash courts, 6 of them, and at the far end of the building a fitness room.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: This is our newest facility, only completed in the spring, but it's already proving to be one of the most popular.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: As well as all these facilities available here on the campus, we also have an arrangement with the local tennis club, which is only 2 miles away, entitling our students to use their courts on weekday mornings in the summer.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: So, I think that there should be something here for everybody, and I hope to see all of you at the center, making use of the facilities.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: If, in the course of the year,
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: you have any suggestions as to how the service we provide might be improved, or its appeal widened, I'll be interested to hear from you.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: For those of you who are up and about early in the morning, we're introducing a 50% morning discount this year.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: This is because the facilities tended to be underused in the mornings last year.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: It means that all the sessions will be half price between 9:00 am and 12:00 noon on weekdays.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So, what exactly are the facilities?
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: What sports can you play here?
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: Well, this room we're in at the moment is called the main hall.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: And it's used mainly for team sports such as football, volleyball and basketball, but also for badminton and aerobics.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: On the other side of the reception area there's the dance studio.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: This provides a smaller, more intimate space, which we use for ballet, modern dance and martial arts, not at the same time of course.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: Then, in a separate building, which you may have noticed on your way here, it's on the other side of the car park, there are the squash courts, 6 of them, and at the far end of the building a fitness room.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: This is our newest facility, only completed in the spring, but it's already proving to be one of the most popular.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: As well as all these facilities available here on the campus, we also have an arrangement with the local tennis club, which is only 2 miles away, entitling our students to use their courts on weekday mornings in the summer.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: So, I think that there should be something here for everybody, and I hope to see all of you at the center, making use of the facilities.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: If, in the course of the year,
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: you have any suggestions as to how the service we provide might be improved, or its appeal widened, I'll be interested to hear from you.
utterances.speaker: JULIE BROOKS

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS OR A NUMBER

items.prefix: Always bring sports <input> when you come to <input> or use the Centre's facilities.

items: 9 am to 10 pm on <input> 10 am to 6 pm on Saturdays

Label the buildings on the map of the Sports Complex.

Label the buildings on the map of the Sports Complex.

test_text.title: Filling in a Competition Entry Form

audioscript.text: Section 3, John Brown, a school student, is entering a competition for young electronic engineers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: He is talking to his teacher Mary Collins.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 21 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Mrs Collins.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: I just wondered if you could help me with this entry form for the Young Electronic Engineer competition.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Hello, John.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Oh, you've made the jigsaw for blind children with the bleeper.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: When they put a piece in correctly, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: OK, let's have a look at the form.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Right, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: I've never filled in one of these before, so...
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Well, let's just do it in pencil first.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: So, name of designers.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Well, Anne helped me with some of the electronics work.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Then you must put her name in as well.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Right...
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Ann Ray.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Sorry, it's ANNE, and her surname is spelled REA.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Good start!
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: OK, REA. And age is easy. you're both 16.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: What have you called the design?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Uh. Keep it short.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: What about jigsaw puzzle design for visually handicapped?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Uh. Too long, just say blind puzzle, that'll do.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: OK, right now size of equipment.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: I've got it noted down here, uh yes, length, uh, sorry, width is 20 centimeters.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: OK, length is 50 centimeters, and then the depth is... well, it's very little.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: What would you say?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Uh. I think you can be approximate.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: I'd say 2.5 cm.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: And the electricity supply, is it mains operated?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: No, it isn't.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: It's actually battery.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: OK, write battery.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: OK, it's the next bit that I'm really not sure what to put.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Well, special features means what is really new about this, you know, suitable for the group you made it for.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Well, it's safe for children.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: That's fine.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Put that in.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: OK, and of course we think it's educational.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Yep, there you are, you've done it.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Anything else?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Well, I think the price is good.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Ha ha, that's probably the most important factor.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: OK, cheap price.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Which brings us on to the next bit.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: What's the cost?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Well, the pieces we made out of old wood, they cost, ooh, $5.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: And the electrics?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: They were more expensive, say, $9.50.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Now, what do they mean by other comments?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: It's just a chance for you to say anything about the equipment and problems you envisage.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Well we'd really like help with making plastic instead of wooden pieces.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Well, put something like, need help to make plastic pieces.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: OK, and the other thing is, we'd like to develop a range of sizes.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Well, that's fine then, just put that.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: And the last bit is when will you send the equipment?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Well, we've got a lot of work on at the moment, and we want to get it as good as we can.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Well, say 25th of June.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Hmm. Can't we make it later?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Well, the last date is the first of July.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Why not say that?
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: OK, that's what I'll put.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: So, that's the lot.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: That's brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Thanks very much, Mrs Collins.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: I'll send it off straight away.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Glad to be of help.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Very best of luck to you both.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN BROWN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: MARY COLLINS

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a talk about ostrich farming.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the talk, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today, I'd like to introduce Ted Hunter, who used to rear sheep and poultry, but who is here to tell us about a rather unusual type of livestock that he's been concentrating on in the last few years.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA

audioscript.text: Ted Hunter is a member of the Domesticated Ostrich Farming Association, and is here to tell us about the possibilities of breeding and rearing these birds in this country.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA

audioscript.text: Thank you, Paula.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Uh. When you look at international restaurant menus and supermarkets, they all tend to feature the same range of meats, beef, lamb, chicken, pork, that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: But people are always interested in something different, and we're now finding that farming can bring new types of meat to our tables.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: The kangaroo is one animal that's now being farmed for its meat, and eaten outside Australia where it comes from.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: It looks and tastes rather like rabbit,
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: though it's slightly darker in color, but it is rather tough, so that's a problem for some people.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Uh. Crocodiles are also being farmed for their meat.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: This is rather like chicken, pale and tender, and it's getting quite fashionable.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Some people also find it's rather fatty, but I think it makes a really tasty sandwich.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Now a third type of meat becoming increasingly available, and the one that I think is by far the nicest of the three is ostrich, which most people say has a similar taste and texture to beef.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: However, it's much better for you than beef, as we'll see later.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Most people think of ostriches as wild animals, but in fact, ostriches have been farmed in South Africa since around 1860.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: At first, they were produced for their feathers.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: In Africa, they were used for tribal ceremonial dress, and they were also exported to Europe and America, where they were made into ladies' fans, and used for decorating hats.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Later, feather fans and big decorated hats went out of fashion, but ostriches were still bred, this time for their hide.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: This can be treated to produce about half a square meter of leather, very delicate, fine stuff, of very good quality.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: At the same time, some of the meat was used for biltong, the air dried strips of meat popular in South Africa as a sort of fast food.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: However, recently there's been more and more interest in the development of ostrich farming in other parts of the world, and more people are recognizing its value as a food source.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Ostrich meat is slightly higher in protein than beef, and much lower in fats and cholesterol.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: It tastes good too.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: A series of European taste tests found that 82% of people prefer ostrich to beef, and one ostrich produces a lot of meat from around 30 to 50 kilograms, mostly from the hind quarters of the bird.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Farmed ostriches don't need African climates, and in fact, ostrich farming is now becoming well established in other parts of the world.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: However, setting up an ostrich farm isn't something to embark on lightly.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Mature breeding birds are very expensive, even a fertilized ostrich egg isn't cheap.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: So you need quite a bit of capital to begin with.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Then the farmer needs special equipment such as incubators for the eggs.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: The young chicks are very dependent on human minders, and need a lot of attention from the people looking after them.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: In addition, ostriches can't be intensively farmed.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: They need space and exercise.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: But in spite of this, they make good farming sense.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: A cow produces only one calf a year, whereas a female ostrich can lay an egg every other day.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: And because the farmers can use incubators and hatched chicks are nourished well and protected from danger, the failure rate on farms is very low indeed, and almost all the fertilized eggs will hatch out into chicks, which will in turn reach maturity.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: This is very different from the situation in the wild, where the vast majority of chicks will die or be killed before they grow up into mature ostriches.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: So it's possible once the initial outlay has been made, for the farmer to be looking at very good profit margins indeed.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: Ostrich farming is still in its early days outside Africa.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: But we hope that ostrich meat will be freely available soon, and before long will be as cheap as beef.
audioscript.speaker: TED

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: At first, they were produced for their feathers.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: In Africa, they were used for tribal ceremonial dress, and they were also exported to Europe and America, where they were made into ladies' fans, and used for decorating hats.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: Later, feather fans and big decorated hats went out of fashion, but ostriches were still bred, this time for their hide.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: This can be treated to produce about half a square meter of leather, very delicate, fine stuff, of very good quality.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: At the same time, some of the meat was used for biltong, the air dried strips of meat popular in South Africa as a sort of fast food.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: However, recently there's been more and more interest in the development of ostrich farming in other parts of the world, and more people are recognizing its value as a food source.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: Ostrich meat is slightly higher in protein than beef, and much lower in fats and cholesterol.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: It tastes good too.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: A series of European taste tests found that 82% of people prefer ostrich to beef, and one ostrich produces a lot of meat from around 30 to 50 kilograms, mostly from the hind quarters of the bird.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: Farmed ostriches don't need African climates, and in fact, ostrich farming is now becoming well established in other parts of the world.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: However, setting up an ostrich farm isn't something to embark on lightly.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: Mature breeding birds are very expensive, even a fertilized ostrich egg isn't cheap.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: So you need quite a bit of capital to begin with.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: Then the farmer needs special equipment such as incubators for the eggs.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: The young chicks are very dependent on human minders, and need a lot of attention from the people looking after them.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: In addition, ostriches can't be intensively farmed.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: They need space and exercise.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: But in spite of this, they make good farming sense.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: A cow produces only one calf a year, whereas a female ostrich can lay an egg every other day.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: And because the farmers can use incubators and hatched chicks are nourished well and protected from danger, the failure rate on farms is very low indeed, and almost all the fertilized eggs will hatch out into chicks, which will in turn reach maturity.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: This is very different from the situation in the wild, where the vast majority of chicks will die or be killed before they grow up into mature ostriches.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: So it's possible once the initial outlay has been made, for the farmer to be looking at very good profit margins indeed.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: Ostrich farming is still in its early days outside Africa.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: But we hope that ostrich meat will be freely available soon, and before long will be as cheap as beef.
utterances.speaker: TED

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Ostrich meat
options: tastes nearly as good as beef.

items.title: One problem with ostrich fanning in Britain is
Ostrich chicks reared on farms
options: must be kept in incubators until mature.

items.title: The speaker suggests ostrich farms are profitable because
options: there is a good market for the meat.

answer_key.correct_answer: fans | feather fans | ladies' fans | ladies' feather fans
leather | delicate leather | fine leather | good quality leather
